content.full_text:
February 3, 1978

Ms. Rosalind C. Cohen
2446 20th Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20009

Dear Ms. Cohen:

Thank you for your letter of January 11, 1978, requesting advice concerning the possible reporting requirements of the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. Before answering your specific questions I think it will be helpful to outline briefly the status of the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. under the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 81000, et seq.). Under the Act, a committee which receives $500 or more in contributions in a calendar year is a Section 82013(a) committee. However, pursuant to regulations adopted by the Commission, there are two different types of Section 82013(a) committees. For purposes of discussion I will refer to these two types of committees as primary purpose committees and multi-purpose committees. A primary purpose committee is a committee formed or existing primarily for political purposes. "Political purposes" means existing for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or the qualification or passage of any measure in the State of California (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18213(a)). On the other hand, a multi-purpose committee is an entity which exists for a variety of purposes one of which is to make contributions or expenditures in connection with California elections.

Based on the information provided in your letter to the Commission, it appears that the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. is a multi-purpose committee because most of the contributions and expenditures involve candidates outside the State of California. Since the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. is a multi-purpose committee, only payments recorded by it which are earmarked for making contributions or expenditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18213(d)). A payment is "earmarked" when at the time of making the payment the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be commingled will be used to make contributions or expenditures relative to California elections. If the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. begins to make contributions to California candidates the employees who contribute to the Fund will have reason to know that their payments or a portion thereof will be used to make contributions in California. Accordingly, a portion of these payments will be contributions received by the Fund.

Ms. Rosalind C. Cohen                    -2-                    February 3, 1978


     A reliable method of determining what portion of a payment from
an employee is a contribution is to allocate on the basis of the
amount of money contributed by the Fund to California candidates.
For example, if the Fund receives $100,000 in employee contributions
and it contributes $19,000 to California candidates, it will have
received $10,000 in "earmarked contributions;" and 10 percent of
each employee contribution will be a contribution within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act. If the Fund does not know exactly how
much it will receive in employee contributions or how much it will
contribute to California candidates, good faith estimates are accept-
able for purposes of prorating. Of course, under this system once
the Fund has contributed $500 or more to California candidates, it
is open will have received $500 in contributions and, therefore, will
be a committee within the meaning of Section §2013(a). If the Women's
Campaign Fund Inc. qualifies as a Section §2013(a) committee it will
be obligated to file a statement of Organization (Form 410) and the
required campaign statements pursuant to Section §4200, etc. The
Fund may terminate its status as a committee under California law at
the time a determination is made that no additional contributions
will be made to California candidates within the foreseeable future.
The Fund would then be obligated to file a termination statement
(Form 415) informing the California Secretary of State that the Fund
has terminated its activity in the state. The Fund may of course
re-register at any time that a decision is made to once again parti-
cipate in California state or local elections.

     I am enclosing for your use and information forms and material which
should be of some assistance to you in your compliance with the
Political Reform Act. Please let me know if I may be of any addi-
tional assistance.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator

RSK:bw

Enclosure

2446 - 20th Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20009
January 11, 1978


Mr. Dan Lowenstein, Chairman
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
925 L Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

     The Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. ("Fund") is a national
non-partisan political committee which supports women candidates
for federal and state elective offices. The Fund, which is
based in Washington, D.C., reports all of its receipts and dis-
bursements to the Federal Election Commission, pursuant to federal
law, and maintains records as required by the Commission.

     As attorney for the Fund, I am seeking your advice con-
cerning the reporting requirements which would be applicable in
the event the Fund made a contribution to a candidate for a
California state office.

     As a matter of practice, the Fund does not identify
candidates which it will support prior to making national solicita-
tion mailings. Thus, its fund-raising is not directed to the
support of specific candidates, but to the support of women candi-
dates who are subsequently selected by the Fund on the basis of
certain criteria. This type of arrangement does not permit the
donation of ear-marked funds or the matching of donors with candi-
dates either specifically or by state. All contributions are
placed in the Fund's general accounts for periodic distribution.

     The Fund has recently incorporated under the laws of
the District of Columbia, for liability purposes only, in
accordance with federal regulations.

     Given this situation, my specific questions are as
follows:

     1.     If the Fund makes no independent expenditure
in connection with California elections and limits its direct

- 2 -

contributions to less than $5,000 in any one calendar year,
would it be exempt from the definition of "committee" under
Government Code § 82013, as amended? (The Fund does solicit
and receive contributions in excess of $500 nationally as
described above, but has not made and does not presently plan
to make special mailings on behalf of one or more California
candidates.)

2.   If the Fund would not be deemed a "committee",
would it be required to file any statements or reports of any
kind, or to satisfy any other state requirements (other than
to identify itself to the candidate or candidates at the
time of any contribution)?

3.   If the Fund is required to file reports, statements, or
literature of any type, how long would it be obligated to con-
tinue doing so assuming that its sole involvement in California
elections would consist of only one or possibly several individual
contributions.

     Because of the proximity of some elections and the
Fund's need to plan its 1978 commitments, I would very much
appreciate your opinion concerning these questions at your
earliest convenience.   I would also appreciate receiving copies
of all applicable forms so as to assure timely compliance by the
Fund with any filing requirements.

     Thank you for your assistance in this matter.  Please
telephone me at (202) 872-6966 if you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        Rosalind C. Cohen

RCC:jac

February 3, 1978

P 78-02235

Mr. Rosalind C. Cohen
2446 29th Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20008

Dear Mr. Cohen:

Thank you for your letter of January 11, 1978, requesting advice concerning the possible reporting requirement of the Women's Campaign
Fund, Inc. Before answering your specific questions I think it will be helpful to outline briefly the status of the Women's Campaign
Fund as a political committee under the Act (52 U.S.C. §431 et seq.). Under the Act, a committee which receives $500 or more in
contributions in a calendar year is a political committee. 2 U.S.C. §431(4)(A). However, pursuant to regulations adopted by the Commission, there are
two different types of Section 8201(i) committees. For purposes of
discussion I will refer to a form of these committees as "single-
purpose committees and multi-purpose committees. A primary purpose
committee is a committee formed or existing primarily for political
purposes, "political purposes" means existing for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or the qualification
or passage of any measure in any state or the District of Columbia
(52 U.S.C. §431(a)). On the other hand, a multi-purpose committee
is an entity which exists for a variety of purposes one of which is
to make contributions or expenditures in connection with California
elections.

Based on the information provided in your letter to the Commission,
it appears that the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. is a multi-purpose
committee because most of the contributions and expenditures involve
candidates outside the State of California. Since the Women's Campaign
Fund, Inc. is a multi-purpose committee, only payments required by it
which are earmarked for making contributions or expenditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of the political reform Act
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(d)). "Payment" when at the time of making the payment the donor knows or has reason
to know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be contributed will be used to make contributions or expenditures related
to California elections. If the Women's Campaign Fund, Inc. begins
to make contributions to California candidates the employees who contribute to its fund will have reason to know that their payments or
a portion thereof will be used to make contributions in California.
Accordingly, a portion of these payments will be contributions received
by the Fund.

To. Rosalind C. Cohen                          -2-                          February 3, 1976


     A reliable method of determining what portion of a payment from
an employee is a contribution is to allocate on the basis of the
amount of money contributed by the Fund to California candidates.
For example, if the Fund received $100,000 in employee contributions
and it contributed $10,000 to California candidates, it will have
received $10,000 in "earmarked contributions;" and 10 percent of
which employee contribution will be a contribution within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act. If the Fund does not know exactly how
much it will receive in employee contributions or how much it will
contribute to California candidates, good faith estimates are reasonable. For purposes of reporting, of course, under this system once
the Fund has contributed $500 or more to California candidates, it
is up to it will have received $500 in contributions. Therefore, it
will be a committee within the meaning of Section 82013(a). If the Women's
Campaign Fund Inc. qualifies as a section 82913(a) committee it will
be obligated to file the Statement of Organization Form 410) and the
required campaign statements pursuant to Section 84200, etc. The
Fund may terminate its status as a committee under California law at
any time a determination is made that no additional contributions
will be made to California candidates within the foreseeable future.
The Fund would then be obligated to file a termination statement
(Form 415) informing the California Secretary of State that the Fund
has terminated its activity in the state. The Fund may of course
re-register at any time that a decision is made to once again participate in California state or local elections.

     I am enclosing for your use and information forms and material which
should be of some assistance to you in your compliance with the
Political Reform Act. Please let me know if I may be of any additional assistance.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator
